one_line_takeaway: Live black-box attacks on top-ranked OpenAI Custom GPTs show 80.2% expert-prompt leakage and 100% tool/knowledge leakage under simple elicitation prompts, and indirect prompt injection (92.2% ASR) and knowledge poisoning (95.4% ASR) induce tool misuse far more reliably than direct chat injection -- while the authors' own prompt-level defensive tokens cut leakage to 0%/14.8% and tool misuse by 83% on average.
key_finding: Basic (single-prompt) expert-prompt leakage succeeds against an overall 80.2% of top GPTs (Table 2; 100% in By ChatGPT and Research & Analysis categories). Against the remaining 'Protected GPTs', among 13 attack variants, classic jailbreaks (DAN, virtual AI simulation, context ignoring) achieve 0% ASR, while Hex injection (63.2%) and the Many-shot prefix attack (84.2%) are by far the most effective (Table 3). Component leakage (tools/knowledge contents) via a single elicitation prompt succeeds with 100% ASR across 3 basic tools, 40 custom tools, and 24 knowledge files, and the AP2 indirect path also achieves 100% ASR. User-uploaded files and developer knowledge files are both stored at the same path, /mnt/data. For tool misuse (Table 4), basic direct-chat attacks yield 33.3%/37.5%/47.7% ASR on DALL-E/Python/Web Browser respectively; among the 13-method corpus, Hex injection (75.8% avg), Structured response (73.6%), and Cognitive overload (62.6%) are most effective, while DAN and virtual AI simulation again yield 0%. Indirect Prompt Injection (AP4) achieves 92.2% average ASR and Knowledge Poisoning (AP5) achieves 95.4% average ASR, both far exceeding direct prompt injection -- the paper's central finding is that non-user-originated instructions (arriving via tool-fetched content or retrieved knowledge) bypass GPT defenses much more reliably than direct chat instructions. The authors' own defensive-token mitigation (Table 5) reduces expert-prompt leakage ASR from 96.3% to 0%, reduces custom-component leakage ASR to an average of 14.8%, and reduces tool-misuse ASR by an average of 83.0% across injection methods (89.2% reduction specifically for knowledge poisoning; Web Browser sees the largest reduction at 88.9%).
core_contribution: An empirical security study of OpenAI's Custom GPTs conducted from a platform-user (runtime-interaction) perspective rather than a static/batch-crawl perspective. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a formal system model of GPTs (Instructions/Expert Prompt P, Knowledge K, Capabilities+Actions/Tools T, and mutable Chat History C) with three adversary vantage points (A0 text-only, A1 external-content, A2 knowledge-uploader) and five formalized attack path templates (AP1-AP5) spanning information leakage (G1) and tool misuse (G2); (2) live black-box attacks executed against real top-ranked GPTs on the GPT Store to measure expert-prompt leakage, component (tool/knowledge) leakage, and tool-misuse attack success rates under a curated 13-method Attack Method Corpus (drawn from prior jailbreak/prompt-injection literature, e.g. DAN, many-shot jailbreak, hex injection, cognitive overload, StruQ-style structured attacks, etc.); (3) reverse-engineering a subset of attacked GPTs (via ActionsGPT/Open API schema reconstruction) to test and quantify the effect of the authors' own proposed prompt-level defensive tokens for both information leakage and tool misuse.
threat_model: Attacker interacts with deployed, live Custom GPTs on the OpenAI GPT Store purely through natural-language conversation and externally-controlled content; no white-box/gradient access, no fine-tuning, fixed-parameter black-box model assumed. Three vantage points are formalized: A0 (text-only attacker sending chat turns, writes only to Chat History C), A1 (attacker who can host/modify external content -- e.g. a VSCode-accessible page reached via ChatGPT's 'Work with Apps' feature -- that a GPT's tools fetch, giving indirect writes into C via tool-returned text), and A2 (attacker who can upload a malicious file into the GPT's Knowledge module K, e.g. a .doc file with embedded instructions). None of the three attackers can directly modify the Expert Prompt P, the Tools T, or existing Knowledge entries -- only elicit disclosure of them or induce misuse through them. Attacker goals: G1 information leakage (exfiltrate P, K, or T contents) and G2 tool misuse (induce policy-violating tool invocations, e.g. generating violent DALL-E images, listing root filesystem contents via Python, or retrieving restricted-content webpages).
limitations: Authors' own stated Threats to Validity: (1) Internal -- LLM output stochasticity/carryover effects were mitigated by running multiple trials, starting each attempt from a fresh chat session, and disabling personalization/memory/model-improvement data flows, but results are still single-model-family (ChatGPT/GPT-4o/GPT-5) and single-platform (GPT Store) observations. (2) External -- representativeness and small sample size: only top-ranked GPTs per category were evaluated (exact N not stated beyond per-tool subsets of 25-48 GPTs in Table 4), and the authors argue but do not empirically verify that this generalizes to the broader long-tail GPT ecosystem. The proposed defenses are explicitly characterized by the authors as 'proof-of-concept,' 'low-overhead, prompt-level mitigations... rather than definitive or optimal solutions,' with no utility/false-refusal trade-off quantified (i.e., no measurement of whether the defensive tokens degrade legitimate task performance). Attack Method Corpus construction relied on Google Scholar citation counts and manual title/abstract filtering, which the authors acknowledge is a secondary, non-exhaustive signal.

datasets_used: Top-ranked GPTs sampled live from the OpenAI GPT Store across its 7 categories (By ChatGPT, Writing, Productivity, Research & Analysis, Education, LifeStyle, DALL-E, Programming); replication package referenced for exact GPT list

Attack Method Corpus: 13 attack methods curated from prior peer-reviewed jailbreak/prompt-injection literature (Table 1), including DAN, JailbreakBench, Many-shot Jailbreaking, Formalizing and Benchmarking Prompt Injection, Nested Jailbreak, Cognitive Overload, Adaptive Suffix Attacks, Hex Injection, Character Injection, and Indirect Prompt Injection / AgentPoison / Imperceptible Content Poisoning sources
40 custom (jit_plugin) Actions tools and 24 knowledge files extracted from leaked expert prompts of top GPTs
Per-tool subsets of 'Tool-invocation Protected GPTs' used for RQ2: 32 DALL-E GPTs, 25 Python GPTs, 34 Web Browser GPTs
quantitative_results: Basic expert-prompt leakage ASR: 80.2% overall (Table 2); 100% for By ChatGPT and Research & Analysis categories, 66.7% for Writing and DALL-E, 83.3% Productivity, 75% Education/LifeStyle/Programming

Protected-GPT expert-prompt leakage by attack variant (Table 3): DAN/Virtual AI simulation/Context ignoring 0%, Adaptive prefix 10.5%, Disguised intent 10.5%, Character injection 21.1%, Fake completion 21.1%, Role play 21.1%, Cognitive overload 26.3%, Structured response 26.3%, Hex injection 63.2%, Many-shot prefix attack 84.2%
Component (tool/knowledge) leakage ASR: 100% via direct chat and 100% via AP2 indirect injection
Basic tool-misuse ASR (direct chat, unprotected GPTs): DALL-E 33.3%, Python 37.5%, Web Browser 47.7%

Attack-variant tool-misuse average ASR (Table 4, protected GPTs): DAN/Virtual AI sim 0%, Disguised intent 5.5%, Context ignoring 16.5%, Adaptive prefix 17.6%, Role play 30.8%, Character injection 39.6%, Many-shot prefix 45.1%, Fake completion 47.3%, Cognitive overload 62.6%, Structured response 73.6%, Hex injection 75.8%
Indirect Prompt Injection (AP4) average ASR: 92.2% (DALL-E 91.7%, Python 97.5%, Web Browser 89.2%)
Knowledge Poisoning (AP5) average ASR: 95.4% (DALL-E 95.8%, Python 97.5%, Web Browser 93.8%)

Defensive-token evaluation (Table 5): expert-prompt leakage ASR reduced from 96.3% to 0% (a 96.3-point drop); custom-component leakage ASR reduced to average 14.8% (drops of 92.6% for prompt injection, 77.8% for indirect injection); tool-misuse ASR reductions averaging 83.0% across methods, with knowledge-poisoning attacks reduced by an average of 89.2% and the Web Browser tool showing the largest reduction (88.9%)

Component distribution among top GPTs (Figure 4): 54.17% use only basic tools, 22.92% use basic+user-defined tools, 9.37% use no tools/knowledge, 8.33% only user-defined tools, 5.21% tools+knowledge; among basic-tool users, 85.5% use Web Browser, 63.2% DALL-E, 55.6% Python
baselines_compared: No external ML baselines -- the paper compares its own set of attack methods against each other (basic single-prompt attack vs. 13 corpus variants vs. indirect prompt injection vs. knowledge poisoning) and compares pre- vs. post-defense ASR on the same reverse-engineered GPTs (self-comparison, not comparison to a prior published defense system)
